original_abstract: Multi-query attention (MQA), which only uses a single key-value head, drastically speeds up decoder inference. However, MQA can lead to quality degradation, and moreover it may not be desirable to train a separate model just for faster inference. We (1) propose a recipe for uptraining existing multi-head language model checkpoints into models with MQA using 5% of original pre-training compute, and (2) introduce grouped-query attention (GQA), a generalization of multi-query attention which uses an intermediate (more than one, less than number of query heads) number of key-value heads. We show that uptrained GQA achieves quality close to multi-head attention with comparable speed to MQA.
humanized_abstract: Multi-query attention (MQA) uses a single key-value head in the decoder and therefore leads to faster decoder inference. Although the output quality of the resulting model may be degraded and faster inference of the current model may not be a motivation to train a separate new model, here we: (1) describe a simple “recipe” for uptraining a model using an existing multiday, multi-head language model checkpoint in less than 5% of the original training compute resources. (2) Introduce the term grouped query attention (GQA) to denote models using a nonunity number of key-value heads, larger than one and less than the number of the query heads as in multi-head attention, a generalization of the MQA, and demonstrate up-trained GQA having only a small loss in model quality compared to mult-head attention while having the faster inference of the corresponding models that use MQA.

(2) Introduce the term grouped query attention (GQA) to denote models using a nonunity number of key-value heads, larger than one and less than the number of the query heads as in multi-head attention, a generalization of the MQA, and demonstrate up-trained GQA having only a small loss in model quality compared to mult-head attention while having the faster inference of the corresponding models that use MQA.
